video_title: David Rosenberg: "There Are No More Bears Left" In This Market
gist: David Rosenberg argues the market's inflation fear is misplaced (productivity is doing 90% of the work, disinflation is coming) while the real risk is a consumer running on a depleted 3% savings rate, an AI-capex bubble with circular financing, and universal bullishness with 'no bears left'. He is defensively positioned: ~50% equities (heavily Asia), ~40% bonds, ~10% gold and miners.

themes.theme: Disinflation vs. the Fed's inflation fixation

insights.claim: David Rosenberg holds an out-of-consensus disinflation call, arguing that after six years of repeated COVID, fiscal, energy and US-Iran war supply shocks, inflation running only ~3.5% is a 'miracle' and should be running much higher if a real inflationary impulse existed.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: If disinflation reasserts, the market's pricing of Fed hikes is wrong and duration/bonds are mispriced to the upside.
insights.quote: It's actually a miracle. It's a miracle that inflation is only running say call it 3 and a half%

insights.claim: Rosenberg frames productivity as structurally disinflationary: 90% of US economic growth in the past year came from productivity vs. 10% from labor input, versus a normal 50/50 split, which he calls 'kryptonite for secular inflation.'
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: A productivity-led expansion argues against sustained inflation and supports lower yields, undercutting the bond-bear case.
insights.quote: considering that productivity is kryptonite for secular inflation

insights.claim: Unit labor costs, which Rosenberg calls 'the mother's milk for future inflation,' have collapsed to 0.5% year-over-year in Q1 from 3% a year earlier, signaling almost no wage-driven inflation pressure.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: Cooling unit labor costs remove the core mechanism for persistent inflation and support the disinflation trade.
insights.quote: unit labor costs which are productivity adjusted wages are running right now year-over-year to the first quarter 0.5% Dan a year ago they were 3%

insights.claim: Commodity and FX moves already point to fading inflation: per Rosenberg oil has almost fully retraced its war run-up, gold is down almost 30%, the CRB is down 13%, and the US dollar is up 6%, yet markets and the Fed keep talking inflation.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: Rolling-over commodities feed into lower headline CPI within months, a setup Rosenberg thinks is underappreciated.
insights.quote: Gold is down almost 30%. The CRB is down 13%. The US dollar is up 6%. You look at the base metals, they rolled over

insights.claim: 10-year TIPS breakeven inflation expectations sit at ~2.2%, lower than before the US-Iran war started, so Rosenberg attributes the rise in market rates to a shift in the Fed reaction function (from two cuts priced to a hike as early as September) rather than any real change in the economy.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: If the higher-rate move is purely Fed-expectation driven and not fundamentals, a dovish repricing would rally bonds sharply.
insights.quote: this run up in market interest rates has mostly reflected the shift in the view on the Fed

insights.claim: Rosenberg predicts a string of negative CPI prints and benign core inflation over the coming months, with airfares and delivery services (oil-linked but counted in core) set to 'come down a lot' after spiking.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: Negative CPI prints would force a dovish Fed repricing and reward bond and rate-cut positioning.
insights.quote: people will be surprised at the string of negative CPI prints we're going to be saying and how benign core is going to be

insights.claim: Rosenberg says there has been no meaningful spillover of the latest energy shock into core inflation or wages, which is what makes the current episode different from 2021-2022 when it filtered into wages and 'it's game over.'
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: Absence of second-round wage effects means the inflation scare is transient, supporting disinflation-linked assets.
insights.quote: once it filters on the wages it's game over. It's tough to get the genie back in the bottle when it morphs into wages

insights.claim: Rosenberg reads Apple's failed price increase (its stock got hammered the day of the announcement) as market evidence that pricing power is breaking and consumers will push back toward competitors like Samsung.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: Eroding corporate pricing power caps the inflation upside and can pressure margins at consumer-facing names.
insights.quote: Apple just tried to raise its prices. They announced a price increase. Their stock got hammered that day

insights.claim: Rosenberg contends the current productivity-driven regime is the opposite of the 1970s inflation era, which was defined by sclerotic productivity and an emboldened, unionized, COLA-protected labor force that does not exist today.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: The structural backdrop argues against a 1970s-style inflation repeat, favoring disinflation trades over inflation hedges.

themes.theme: Consumer fragility and the savings-rate time bomb

insights.claim: The US personal savings rate has fallen to 3% from ~5% a year ago and ~8% pre-COVID, which Rosenberg calls the single biggest and least-discussed source of stimulus for the US economy and a classic mean-reverting series (Bob Ferrell rule one).
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: A mean-reversion of the savings rate toward 8% would crush consumer spending and could trigger a consumer recession that catches markets off guard.
insights.quote: that decline in the savings rate ... actually singularly is the biggest source of stimulus for the US economy that nobody talks about

insights.claim: Rosenberg argues real disposable income growth is running at 0% year-over-year while real consumer spending runs north of 2%, a gap financed by the high end spending equity gains and the low end tapping credit cards, which he views as unsustainable.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: A spending level unsupported by income is fragile; any hit to wealth or credit access converts directly into a consumer downturn.
insights.quote: real disposable income growth is running at 0% on the nose in the past year

insights.claim: Rosenberg warns the low-end consumer is in extreme stress with a re-acceleration in credit card usage at 20% interest on a $1.3 trillion unpaid balance and a delinquency rate at a two-decade high of 15%, yet issuers keep extending more credit.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: Rising delinquencies plus continued credit extension is a late-cycle setup; a tightening in lending standards would remove a key spending prop.
insights.quote: The delinquency rate is at a two decade high of 15%, but still somehow the issuers are still giving the low-end household more credit cards to use up

insights.claim: Rosenberg estimates that if the consumer were forced to spend within their means, real consumer spending would be ~0% and real GDP growth barely 1%, meaning the economy is only about one percentage point from stall speed.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: A thin margin above stall speed means a small shock to spending flips the narrative from boomlet to recession.
insights.quote: real GDP growth would be barely 1% ... 1 percentage point from where we are today is all it takes

insights.claim: Rosenberg attributes the first-half consumer resilience partly to a one-off ~$50 billion decline in the consumer tax bill (Jan-May) from stepped-up income tax refunds, which he says was fully spent and is now in the rearview mirror.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: With the tax tailwind exhausted, second-half consumer spending loses a support that isn't repeatable.
insights.quote: the tax bill for the consumer sector broadly speaking January to May was down $50 billion All that money got spent

insights.claim: Rosenberg identifies an equity wealth effect driving spending, noting household high-end savings rates may be near zero as people treat 401k and brokerage gains as permanent, an analog to the late-1990s and the 2006-2007 housing-wealth spending.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: If equities stall, the wealth-effect spending unwinds, making consumer spending highly reflexive to the stock market.
insights.quote: we may have a situation where the savings rate for the high-end consumer could be zero right now

themes.theme: Labor market: trend vs. monthly noise

insights.claim: Rosenberg argues the labor market moves glacially in trend and that year-over-year non-farm payrolls are running flat while household-survey employment is at minus 0.3%, so month-to-month stabilization claims are misleading.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: A flat-to-negative employment trend contradicts the 'resilient labor market' narrative and supports a weaker growth/dovish-Fed path.
insights.quote: on a year-over-year basis the non-farm payrolls are running flat ... the employment of the household survey to the decimal place is running minus.3%

insights.claim: Rosenberg says the 4.3% unemployment rate understates slack because it is held down by a falling labor-force participation rate; absent that drop, unemployment would be ~5.1%.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: More hidden slack means weaker wage pressure and a case for Fed cuts rather than hikes.
insights.quote: it's only 4.3% because of the drop in the labor force participation rate which if it hadn't gone down ... would be at 5.1

insights.claim: Rosenberg cautions that BLS benchmark revisions repeatedly mark down first-release payrolls by the third estimate, so traders acting on strong initial prints are trading a number that later gets revised sharply lower.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: Discount hot payroll prints; the confirmed trend is weaker, which favors patience over chasing rate-hike pricing.
insights.quote: most of the time in the past year the non-firm imperial numbers by the time you get the third estimate were lower than they were at the first estimate

themes.theme: No bears left: concentration and sentiment risk

insights.claim: Rosenberg's central market worry is universal bullishness and herd mentality, citing that 'there are no bears left,' portfolio-manager cash ratios are down to 1%, and household balance sheets hold 73% in equities versus under 7% in bonds.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: Extreme positioning with no cash buffer is fuel for an outsized drawdown on any redemption or shock.
insights.quote: There are no bears left. There are no bears left.

insights.claim: Rosenberg notes S&P concentration in the top 10 stocks and in tech-plus-telecom is now higher than at the late-1990s bubble peak, with 90% of past-year earnings growth and 70% of market-cap expansion coming from tech and telecom.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: Record concentration means index-level returns are hostage to a handful of names; a rotation out of tech drags the whole market.
insights.quote: the concentration of the top 10 stocks and the concentration of tech and telecom in the S&P is higher now than it was at the bubble peak back in the late 1990s

insights.claim: Rosenberg says a negative equity risk premium (Shiller CAPE at 41 implies a 2.4% real earnings yield versus a 2.7% real long-bond yield) is the market telling investors equities are no longer treated as a risky asset class, echoing the late 1990s.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: A negative ERP has historically preceded poor forward equity returns; it argues for trimming equity risk despite momentum.
insights.quote: When the ERP is negative, the markets are telling you and I that equities uh are no longer a risky asset class

insights.claim: Rosenberg invokes the Charles Merrill 1928 story, where Merrill raised cash 10 months before the crash against his own board and was told to see a psychiatrist, to argue that being early on risk management saves capital and that today's market is 'more out of control now than they were in 1928.'
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: Reframes 'too early' as prudent risk management, supporting de-risking now even if the top is months away.
insights.quote: I would say things are more out of control now than they were in 1928. Okay? In every respect

insights.claim: Rosenberg cites the market truism that bull markets are 'an escalator going up' while bear markets are 'an elevator going down,' noting historically about three-quarters of the prior bull market gets reversed quickly in a bear market.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: Asymmetric downside speed means investors who fail to take profits early can give back 75% of gains fast.
insights.quote: Bull markets are an escalator going up in time and a bare market in a bare market is an elevator going down

insights.claim: Rosenberg highlights that nobody has taken profits or rebalanced, evidenced by household equity asset share at a record 73%, leaving portfolios maximally exposed when the reversal comes.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: Unrebalanced, all-in portfolios amplify downside; a contrarian would trim equities and add bonds/cash now.
insights.quote: Nobody has rebalanced. Nobody's taken profits. Nobody has recalibrated their portfolio.

themes.theme: AI capex bubble, rotation, and how it ends

insights.claim: Rosenberg argues the AI theme has spread far beyond tech and telecom into utilities, Caterpillar and consumer discretionary (via Amazon), so true market concentration in the single AI-data-center macro theme is much higher than the ~40% tech-and-telecom share suggests.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: Broader-than-labeled AI exposure means an AI-capex reversal would hit sectors investors think are diversified.
insights.quote: maybe Caterpillar is today's Corning. Caterpillar all of a sudden is become a derivative of the AI trade

insights.claim: Rosenberg says a rotation within tech has driven parabolic gains in memory/storage and second-tier semis, citing Corning (GLW) up ~700% in 13-14 months and the SOX up 100% year-to-date, mostly since mid-April, which he calls 'obviously a bubble.'
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: Parabolic non-Mag-7 semis are the current mania leg; identifying the bubble's location helps time where the damage lands next.

insights.claim: Rosenberg predicts the AI unwind proceeds in stages like 1999-2001: hyperscaler stocks roll over first on overspending fears, then semiconductors follow, with the trigger being news flow that is merely 'less good' rather than outright bad (the 2001 Cisco-miss analog).
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: Watch for a bellwether earnings 'less good' moment as the sequencing signal that the semi leg is about to break.
insights.quote: all that has to happen, okay, is that the news flow does not match the broad expectation. It just has to be less good.

insights.claim: Rosenberg flags that historically asset-light tech companies have become capital-intensive and are now burning cash and being compelled to raise debt and equity for the first time, even as valuations haven't adjusted to this changed complexion.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: Cash-burning tech forced into capital markets is a regime change that raises funding/liquidity risk not yet in valuations.
insights.quote: For the first time, we are seeing cash being burnt and companies being compelled to come to the debt and equity markets to raise money. They didn't have to do that before.

insights.claim: Rosenberg points out old-economy capex is in its own recession (non-tech capex negative, commercial construction contracting) even as tech-related capex is up double digits in real terms, masked by AI's dominance of the headline.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: A narrow, AI-concentrated capex boom over a broad capex recession means growth is fragile if AI spending slows.
insights.quote: old economy capbacks is actually in a recession of its own. Commercial construction is in a recession of its own.

insights.claim: Rosenberg argues the real second-order risk is that hyperscaler spending pledges partially unwind, creating a ripple through semiconductors via pullbacks in orders and spending plans, with data-center pushouts and delays already appearing.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: An order/spending pullback is the transmission mechanism from AI-capex doubts to a broad semi and market selloff.

insights.claim: Rosenberg emphasizes that credit always leads equities, sometimes by a year, citing that mortgage-credit problems preceded the October 2007 S&P peak by about 10 months.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: Watch credit spreads, not equities, for the earliest warning of a market top.
insights.quote: credit always leads. Credit always leads the equity bark. and sometimes by a year.

insights.claim: Rosenberg flags that BB (non-investment-grade) quality spreads have widened out dramatically as a canary in the coal mine, while multiple private-credit firms are gating their funds and capping redemptions amid general risk-on exuberance.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: Widening junk spreads plus private-credit gating during euphoria is a classic pre-turn divergence to monitor closely.
insights.quote: Take a look at what's happened to CoubleB spreads. They have widened out dramatically. And that's usually a canary in the coal mine.

insights.claim: Rosenberg warns of redemption risk if portfolio managers at 1% cash face equity outflows, drawing a parallel to private-credit funds already capping redemptions and asking what happens when the same dynamic hits equities.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: With near-zero cash buffers, a redemption wave would force forced selling and accelerate any equity drawdown.
insights.quote: God forbid if we ever have a redemption event. Imagine if whatever happens, imagine if that happens in equities.

insights.claim: Rosenberg (prompted by Nathan) frames the AI buildout's financialization as dangerously circular, citing a reported ~$35B Blackstone-Apollo SPV to finance Google TPUs designed by Broadcom and used by Anthropic, plus Nvidia funding tens of billions of its own customers.
insights.speaker: guest and host
insights.so_what: Circular vendor/customer financing inflates apparent demand and concentrates systemic risk into private credit, insurers and pensions.

insights.claim: Rosenberg likens today's interconnected AI financing to the Korean chaebol structure and the late-1990s telecom/fiber vendor-financing web, warning the circular arrangement is at least as big a concentration risk as in 1999.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: The 1999 telecom analog suggests circular financing can unravel quickly once one link (a lender or customer) pulls back.
insights.quote: it almost resembles you know the the old uh chable uh industries back in Korea when when when everything is is intertwined with each other

insights.claim: Rosenberg argues US depreciation accounting under the 'one big beautiful bill' flatters reported earnings: the seller books full revenue while the buyer fully expenses in year one, and adjusting for the depreciation allowance puts the S&P P/E at 30.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: If the earnings backdrop is a depreciation-driven mirage, the market is far more expensive than it screens, undercutting the bull case.
insights.quote: when you adjust for the depreciation allowance effect, did you know that the price earnings multiple on the S&P is actually 30?

insights.claim: Rosenberg concludes the earnings backdrop is 'less bullish than it appears,' being both highly concentrated (90% from tech) and inflated by circular arrangements and depreciation accounting, though he stops short of calling it bearish.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: Lower-quality, concentrated earnings mean the market's fundamental support is weaker than consensus assumes.
insights.quote: the earnings backdrop is probably even less bullish than it appears. I'm not going to say it's bearish. It's just less bullish.

insights.claim: Despite his perma-bear label, Rosenberg is roughly 50% equities in his model portfolio, but sector- and region-specific, and is notably bullish on Asia, where he says he more than made up for what he missed in US equities.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: Signals a rotation trade out of concentrated US mega-cap tech into Asian and low-beta equities.
insights.quote: So what I missed in the US I more than made up for in Asia which we are actually very bullish on.

insights.claim: Rosenberg holds about half his portfolio in bonds and roughly 10% in gold and miners, betting that once Fed rate-hike expectations leave the system, US rates fall relative to the world, the dollar weakens, and gold reverses its recent underperformance.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: A concrete dovish-repricing trade: long duration, long gold/miners, short dollar as the Fed shifts.
insights.quote: a nice chunk of it probably 10% is in gold and the miners because I ... think that once the Fed rate hike expectations come out of the system interest rates will fall

insights.claim: Rosenberg says his low-beta, high-Sharpe model portfolio (the genesis for a forthcoming Global Macro ETF) is up more than 60% over the past three-plus years, evidence a defensive, non-100%-US-equity approach can outperform.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: Track record supports the pitch that diversified, low-beta positioning beats crowding into US mega-cap equities.
insights.quote: it's up more than 60% in the past three plus years

insights.claim: Rosenberg maintains an out-of-consensus disinflation-linked book on the premise the Fed will not hike as the market expects, positioning for rate cuts to eventually be repriced in.
insights.speaker: David Rosenberg
insights.so_what: If the Fed disappoints hawkish pricing, front-end and duration rally, rewarding the bond-heavy stance.
insights.quote: I don't think they're hiking rates like the market thinks.
